q: Shakespeare's Globe Rose theatre Park Street
w: Sam Wanamaker's faithful reconstruction of Shakespeare's open-air playhouse — thatched, timber-framed and standing close to the original, opened in 1997 after his decades-long crusade. A few steps away, marked under glass, lie the foundations of the Rose, the first Bankside theatre of 1587.

A mile of tunnels dug 40 metres below High Holborn in the early 1940s as a bomb shelter, later home to a Cold War telephone exchange, a government 'citadel' and the rumoured Q-Whitehall hotline. Long sealed and mythologised; plans to open them to visitors are slowly advancing. (No public access yet.)

The underground command bunker from which Churchill ran the Second World War, left almost exactly as it was on VE Day and now part of the Imperial War Museum. The Map Room's pin-stuck walls; the secret transatlantic hotline to Roosevelt hidden behind a door marked as a toilet — history frozen mid-crisis.

q: Down Street disused tube station Mayfair
w: A Piccadilly line station that opened in 1907 in too-rich Mayfair to ever pay, and closed in 1932 — then secretly became Churchill's wartime refuge before the War Rooms were ready, and the Railway Executive's HQ. The oxblood-tiled façade still stands on Down Street; trains roar past the bricked-up platforms.

A vast deep-level air-raid shelter (1940–42) with bunks for 8,000 people far below the Northern line. In 1948 it housed the first arrivals from the Empire Windrush, sleeping underground while they sought work and lodging in Brixton nearby — the birth of multicultural London, beneath it.

A dark, twisting one-way tunnel-street curving down beneath the Adelphi off the Strand — a survivor of the Adam brothers' 18th-century riverside scheme, which cabbies nickname the 'Bat Cave'. Damp, sodium-lit and faintly sinister; one of central London's last genuinely hidden streets.

q: Barnard's Inn Hall Gresham College Holborn
w: A rare medieval hall tucked behind shopfronts off Holborn — a 14th-century survivor with a 16th-century timber roof, once an Inn of Chancery and a setting in Dickens's Great Expectations. Now home to Gresham College, which has given free public lectures since 1597; slip in and sit under the rafters.

q: Cafe in the Crypt St Martin in the Fields
w: Lunch among the dead: a vaulted café in the 18th-century crypt of St Martin-in-the-Fields on Trafalgar Square, where you eat over the worn ledger-stones of the buried. Brick arches, candle-glow and a brass-rubbing studio next door; an atmospheric everyday detour beneath the church steps.

q: Hill Garden and Pergola Hampstead Heath
w: An Edwardian fantasy on the edge of Hampstead Heath — a raised, vine-draped pergola walk built for the soap magnate Lord Leverhulme's garden parties, now romantically half-ruined and free to wander. Wisteria, stone columns and long views; the most quietly cinematic garden in north London.

n: The Two Mice of Philpot Lane
q: Two Mice Philpot Lane Monument cheese
w: London's smallest public sculpture: two tiny mice nibbling cheese, carved high on a building corner at Philpot Lane near the Monument. Legend says they remember two workmen who fell to their deaths brawling over a stolen lunch while building the Monument itself. Easy to walk past forever.

q: Bovril ghost sign Effra Road Brixton
w: A vast faded advertisement for Bovril, painted on a flank wall above Effra Road by Windrush Square and dating from perhaps the 1920s — one of London's best-loved ghost signs, slowly dissolving into the brick. Look up; it's easy to walk under and never notice.

q: Hovis ghost sign Inchmery Road Catford
w: An enormous hand-painted Hovis advertisement on the flank of a building off Inchmery Road in Catford — a rare ghost sign for a brand that still exists. The 'wall dogs' who painted these mixed their pigment with linseed oil to make it last; a century on, it just barely has.

q: 223 Kilburn High Road ghost sign Criterion Gillette
w: At 223 Kilburn High Road two ghost adverts bleed through one another — Criterion Matches beneath, Gillette Razors above — a palimpsest of early-20th-century brands sharing one weathered wall. The clearest example in London of signs painted over signs.

q: 104 New Cross Road ghost sign Nestle
w: On 104 New Cross Road three different painted adverts overlap, the most legible a Nestlé Milk sign — layers of a vanished high street's commerce, one brushed over the next as brands and decades changed. A whole archaeology of advertising on a single gable.

q: Player's Please ghost sign Kenworthy Road Hackney
w: 'Player's Please' — the old slogan for Player's Navy Cut cigarettes — still faintly legible on a wall on Kenworthy Road in Hackney. A snapshot of an era when tobacco was painted ten feet tall on the side of an ordinary street, long before any health warning.

s: a printer in a hidden court
q: Snashall Son ghost sign Wardrobe Place
w: Tucked in Wardrobe Place — a tiny cobbled courtyard off Carter Lane in the shadow of St Paul's — a faded sign for 'Snashall & Son, Printers, Stationers & Account Book Manufacturers' clings to the brick. The quietest, most central ghost sign in the City.

q: Carreras Cigarette Factory Greater London House Mornington Crescent
w: A startling 1928 Art Deco temple on Mornington Crescent, built for Carreras (makers of Black Cat cigarettes) in full Egyptian Revival — once flanked by two giant black bronze cats and a solar disc. Now offices as 'Greater London House'; the restored cats still guard the door.

s: Art Deco on a Tudor hall
q: Eltham Palace English Heritage Art Deco
w: Henry VIII's boyhood palace — then, in the 1930s, the millionaire Courtaulds grafted a dazzling Art Deco mansion straight onto its surviving medieval Great Hall. Gold-leaf bathroom, a glass-domed entrance hall, and centrally-heated quarters built for Mah-Jongg, their pet ring-tailed lemur.

A breathtaking traditional Hindu mandir rising out of suburban Neasden — carved from thousands of tonnes of Italian and Bulgarian limestone and Indian marble, shaped by hand in India and assembled here without a single beam of structural steel. One of the largest of its kind outside India.

q: Strawberry Hill House Horace Walpole Twickenham
w: Horace Walpole's 'little Gothic castle' of 1749 — a snow-white confection of battlements, pinnacles and papier-mâché vaulting that all but invented the Gothic Revival and inspired the first Gothic novel. Theatrical, asymmetrical and gloriously strange on the Twickenham riverside.

s: a Tudor grandstand in the forest
q: Queen Elizabeth's Hunting Lodge Chingford Epping Forest
w: A three-storey Tudor timber grandstand of 1543 on the edge of Epping Forest, built so Henry VIII's court could watch — and shoot — the deer driven below. Despite the name, Elizabeth I only inherited it; an immaculate, surreal survivor among the ancient hornbeams.

A towering brick tower mill of 1854, one of the last in Greater London — and improbably marooned amid a quiet Croydon housing estate. Round a dull suburban corner and a giant Victorian windmill rears up; restored and occasionally open, a quintessential flâneur double-take.

Flaky, inventive Danishes and the famous bacon-maple morning bun — Pophams grew from a tiny Prince of Wales Road room in 2017 into Islington and Hackney all-day bakery-diners. Come for cardamom buns and a flat white; the laminated pastry is the draw.

Born in a Dalston shipping container in 2014, the Dusty Knuckle is part bakery, part social enterprise training young people facing barriers — and the maker of London's cult focaccia sandwiches. Serious sourdough, fat fillings, perpetual queues.

Under a London Fields railway arch since 2010, E5 Bakehouse helped spark East London's sourdough obsession — milling its own grain, baking the much-imitated Hackney Wild loaf and roasting coffee in the same warm, floury room.

q: St John Bakery Druid Street doughnut
w: The bakery arm of Fergus Henderson's nose-to-tail St John, in a Druid Street railway arch — home of the legendary custard doughnut that launched a thousand imitators. Eccles cakes, fennel sausage rolls and bread baked through the night.

Opened by the founders of Frieze magazine and named for Alice B. Toklas, Gertrude Stein's partner — an art-world bakery near the Strand turning out impeccable viennoiserie and Mediterranean plates in a serene courtyard space.

Jolene mills much of its own flour and buys from regenerative farmers, blurring bakery and neighbourhood diner. A Newington Green favourite for blistered pastries, natural wine and seasonal plates from a tiny open kitchen.

Started as a lockdown side-project and grew into one of West London's most dependable bun fixes — gooey cinnamon and cardamom knots, pillowy and gloriously over-proofed. A reliable Notting Hill sugar hit.

A community-minded, feng-shui-calm matcha bar sourcing ceremonial grade direct from Kagoshima farms — and rationing carefully through the 2024–25 matcha shortage. Whisked usucha, matcha lattes and a quiet Marylebone room to drink them in.

q: Kova Patisserie Soho matcha crepe cake
w: Kova built a cult around its matcha mille-crêpe cake — dozens of paper-thin crêpes layered with green-tea cream. A Soho counter where the Japanese-French sweets are as photogenic as they are good.

A tiny Covent Garden café with a strict 'only orderers inside' rule that keeps it monastically calm — Korean-inflected matcha, carefully whisked, in a room built for slowing down. Cult following, very few seats.

q: frothee matcha King's Cross Caledonian Road
w: A minimalist, matcha-first café off Caledonian Road where the powder is weighed to the gram and whisked fresh to order. No frills, properly frothy — for people who take their usucha seriously.

q: WA Cafe matcha Japanese patisserie London
w: A Tokyo-style patisserie (born, improbably, in Ealing) with a no-WiFi, sit-and-savour ethos — delicate Japanese cakes, matcha and pour-overs in a hushed Marylebone room that wants your attention, not your laptop.
Named for the Japanese word for 'flowing,' and Bruce Lee's 'be water' — a Marylebone coffee-and-matcha bar with a serene, design-led fit-out and seriously sourced green tea. Flow in, slow down.
A jewel-box Islington tea house where matcha feels genuinely ceremonial — served among antique ceramics, dried flowers and wagashi sweets in a hidden room above an antique shop. Tea as quiet theatre.

q: P Franco 107 Lower Clapton natural wine
w: A former Lower Clapton grocer's that became the spiritual home of London natural wine — bottles on the shelf, a single hot-plate kitchen turning out brilliant small plates, elbow-to-elbow with strangers. Now trading as '107'; the legend stands.

Small but perfectly formed, with a cheeky 1980s-style logo masking a serious low-intervention list — Top Cuvée pours adventurous glasses and plates ambitious food in a snug Highbury room, and ships bottles nationwide too.

s: The bar that changed London wine
q: Sager and Wilde Hackney Road wine bar
w: When it opened in a former East End boozer in 2013, the green-tiled Sager + Wilde helped rewrite how London drinks wine — a long marble bar, fair mark-ups and grilled-cheese to match. A genuine scene-shifter on Hackney Road.

Bar Crispin shrugs off the usual wine-bar polish — interiors somewhere between grown-up kebab shop and Mediterranean canteen — and pours a sharp, low-intervention list beside excellent small plates. Anti-aesthetic, seriously good.

q: Diogenes the Dog Elephant Castle wine
w: Named for the Greek Cynic who lived in a barrel, this neighbourhood wine bar near Elephant & Castle pours a thoughtful, characterful list with warm service — proof the good stuff doesn't only live in the centre.

A pocket-sized Nunhead deli and wine shop that sells continental provisions by day and turns into a buzzing little wine bar by night — cheese, charcuterie and low-intervention bottles among the shelves. Neighbourhood gold.

q: Sons and Daughters sandwich Coal Drops Yard
w: From James Ramsden and Sam Herlihy of Pidgin, a Coal Drops Yard counter that treats the sandwich as high craft — posh-nostalgia fillings on properly good bread, made to order. King's Cross lunch as art.

q: Dom's Subs Hackney Mare Street Rasputin's
w: Dom's brought New Jersey-style Italian-American subs to Hackney and Time Out crowned them London's best — towering, properly dressed, gloriously messy. The standalone shops closed in 2026; the subs now fly out of its Mare Street bar, Rasputin's.

Max Halley has been elevating the humble sandwich in Crouch Hill since 2014 — dictionary-thick, gleefully overloaded creations washed down with pints. So iconic that fashion houses have come knocking; still defiantly unpretentious.
A Bristol export that went viral and now packs out the City at lunch — fronted by an evangelical owner and built on fat, fresh, no-nonsense fillings. Office-lunch theatre, with the queue to prove it.

q: Paul Rothe and Son Marylebone deli
w: A Marylebone family deli running since 1900, all wooden shelves and old jars, where sandwiches are built to order from whatever's good that day. A genuine time capsule among the boutiques — soup, a sarnie and a chat.

The yellow-signed 'original' at 155 Brick Lane claims a beigel trade here since 1855 — a date most historians treat as contested marketing (the present shop dates to the early 1960s). It briefly closed in 2024, then reopened; the rivalry with the white one next door is half the fun.

q: Panzer's Deli St John's Wood bagel
w: A St John's Wood institution since the 1940s — a groaning Jewish deli of smoked salmon, rye, pickles and bagels, with fruit and provisions spilling onto the pavement. North London's grand old-school grocer.

q: M Manze Tower Bridge Road pie mash
w: Generally said to be London's oldest surviving pie-and-mash shop, at 87 Tower Bridge Road since 1902 — marble counters, eel-and-liquor and green parsley sauce ladled with ritual. A working monument to Cockney eating.

q: F Cooke Hoxton Street pie mash
w: The Cooke family have served pie and mash since Fred Cooke first opened in 1862 — the Hoxton Street shop a glorious survivor of tiled walls, eels and liquor. Eel-and-pie London, still trading against the odds.
A family pie business going since 1890 and a Greenwich fixture a short walk from the Cutty Sark — pie, mash and liquor served to tourists and locals alike. Comfort food with a 130-year pedigree.

Arment's opened its first Walworth shop in 1914; the Westmoreland Road branch is the area's last survivor — eel, pie, mash and liquor in a neighbourhood that once had dozens. A vanishing South London ritual.

A Bethnal Green institution since 1900, its Grade II-listed 1946 marquetry interior carved by a regular — the Pellicci family still working the counter, the Krays once at the tables. The platonic ideal of the Italian-London caff.

Opened in 1946, this black-tiled Westminster classic is a film-location darling you'll recognise from a dozen movies — fry-ups barked across the room, formica, and a queue of cabbies and civil servants. Greasy-spoon perfection.

q: Terry's Cafe Great Suffolk Street Borough
w: Set up by Terry in 1982 — son of a pastry cook and an Army chef — and now run by his son, a proudly old-school British caff off Great Suffolk Street. Bubble and squeak, doorstep toast and Union Jacks; comfort with a wink.

The gelateria across the road from chef Jacob Kenedy's Bocca di Lupo, raiding the Italian repertoire for ricotta-and-sour-cherry, fresh-mint stracciatella and warm bombolini. Soho's best scoop, and a cup of proper hot chocolate in winter.

Christian Oddono drew on a family with a century of Italian gelato-making to open this South Kensington favourite in 2004 — dense, intense, natural-ingredient gelato that regularly wins national awards. The pistachio is the test.

q: Ruby Violet King's Cross ice cream
w: One of London's few true scoop-shops making everything in-house, in the Midland Goods Shed at King's Cross — wildly inventive flavours, torched-meringue sundaes and a menu that doesn't take itself too seriously.

A small-batch, fiercely seasonal gelato maker beloved by chefs, working out of a Bermondsey arch — flavours that shift with the market (blood orange, fennel, fig-leaf). Industry-insider ice cream you queue for at markets.

Founded in 2019 by three Hong Kong-raised childhood friends — the 'three uncles' — serving glistening Cantonese roast meats (char siu, crispy pork) over rice and proper siu mei. Born in Brixton Village; the real deal.

q: Xi'an Impression Highbury Arsenal biang biang
w: A shoebox Shaanxi restaurant on Benwell Road, directly opposite Arsenal's Emirates — hand-pulled biang-biang noodles, cumin-lamb 'burgers' and chilli-slicked dumplings. Tiny, cash-friendly and worshipped on match days.

A Chinatown stalwart where chefs hand-fold Shanghai-style xiao long bao behind a glass screen — soup dumplings, dim sum and late hours in the heart of the West End. Watch the pleating, then burn your mouth happily.

London's temple of cult cinema since 1962, tucked behind Leicester Square — all-night marathons, sing-along screenings, 35mm rarities and a gleefully film-obsessed crowd. The anti-multiplex, and proud of it.

Built in 1910 and showing films since 1912, the Phoenix is one of Britain's oldest continuously-running cinemas — a barrel-vaulted East Finchley gem with original plasterwork. A picture house that has simply never stopped.

Often called the birthplace of British cinema: in 1896 the Lumière brothers showed Britain its first moving pictures here, beneath what's now the University of Westminster. Beautifully restored, it screens arthouse and classics where the medium began.

A Kingsland High Street picture palace with roots to 1909 and a glorious 1930s Art Deco auditorium — independent, community-owned and a Dalston landmark. Late films, festivals and a balcony worth the ticket alone.

A 1913 cinema that sat derelict for decades until a community crowdfunder brought it roaring back in 2017 — now a beloved Homerton two-screener with a bar and a fierce local following. Resurrection, one pledge at a time.

q: The Lexi Cinema Kensal Rise charity
w: The UK's first social-enterprise cinema — charity-run and volunteer-staffed in Kensal Rise, donating its profits to a South African township project. A single screen with a conscience, and a properly good programme.

An independent arthouse that opened in 2022 on Parker Street — all velvet, warm lighting and thoughtful repertory programming. A plush, grown-up antidote to the multiplex, hidden in plain sight near Holborn.

The biggest of the Rough Trade shops, inside the Old Truman Brewery off Brick Lane — vast racks, in-store gigs, coffee and a wall of new vinyl. The flagship of the label that gave punk and post-punk a home.

q: Sounds of the Universe Soho records
w: Born as a Camden market stall, now a Broadwick Street cornerstone and home of the brilliant Soul Jazz label — reggae, funk, house, library music and reissues for serious diggers. Soho's deepest record room.
A second-hand institution on Berwick Street since 1984 — and a slice of Britpop history, its shopfront immortalised on the cover of Oasis's Morning Glory. Browse the racks where the photo was shot.

A North London institution since 1997, the Essex Road flagship hides a basement trove beneath a well-curated ground floor — secondhand and new across every genre, generously priced. A proper afternoon's dig.
A cosy Greenwich independent specialising in 60s and 70s pop, rock, soul, funk and cult soundtracks — the kind of small, knowledgeable shop where you go in for one record and leave with five.

Built in 1872 with a sumptuous 1891 interior, this Grade II*-listed High Holborn pub keeps its rare Victorian 'snob screens' and etched-glass snugs intact — drinking in a gin-palace that time forgot, at Sam Smith's prices.

A High Holborn pub with one of the longest bars in Britain, soaring triangular roof trusses and a row of cosy wooden cubicles — a vast, cathedral-like hall on a site that's served drinkers since the 15th century.

s: Haunted duel pub, notes on the ceiling
q: The Grenadier Belgravia ghost Wilton Row
w: Hidden down a Belgravia mews on Wilton Row, this 1818 pub began as an officers' mess — sentry box still outside, ceiling papered with banknotes, and a famous (if unprovable) ghost. A duel-haunted bolt-hole behind the grand houses.

Established in 1602 — fourteen years before Shakespeare died — this tiny pub behind the Royal Courts of Justice survived the Great Fire and still pulls in barristers and a famously well-dressed pub cat. Four centuries of legal gossip.

q: Sky Garden Fenchurch Street free booking
w: The free public garden atop the 'Walkie-Talkie' at 20 Fenchurch Street — three storeys of palms and lookout terraces on the 35th floor, with sweeping Thames views. Book a (free) slot ahead; arrive for sunset if you can.

Opened in 2023 on the 58th floor of 22 Bishopsgate, Horizon 22 is Europe's highest free public viewing gallery — 254 metres up, looking down on the Shard and the whole City sprawl. Free, but book ahead.

The City's largest public roof garden, on the 15th floor of Fen Court — wisteria walkways, a fountain and open-air views of the surrounding towers, with no booking and no charge. The easy, uncrowded rooftop.

q: One New Change roof terrace St Paul's
w: A free sixth-floor roof terrace atop the One New Change centre, delivering an unusually intimate, eye-level view of St Paul's dome — close enough to count the stonework. The best free St Paul's photo in London.

A protected viewpoint on Hampstead Heath whose sightline to St Paul's is shielded by law — kite-fliers, swimmers and the whole city laid out below. The classic, free, no-queue London panorama; bring a flask.

q: General Wolfe statue Greenwich Park view
w: The terrace by the General Wolfe statue at the top of Greenwich Park frames the Old Royal Naval College, the Thames and Canary Wharf in one sweep — Wren's baroque maritime London with the towers rising behind. Unforgettable at dusk.

A Gothic triangular folly built in 1784 in Oxleas Wood on Shooter's Hill, commemorating a colonial siege — and, on clear days, offering views over several counties from its tower. A genuinely secret South-East London lookout in the trees.

Opened in 1906, this is the UK's largest freshwater pool by surface area — a vast, cold, gloriously unheated expanse ringed by candy-coloured changing cubicles. South London's great democratic swim, beloved by hardy year-round bathers.
A Grade II-listed 1938 lido on the edge of Hampstead Heath, famous for its gleaming stainless-steel pool — bracingly unheated and open year-round. Art Deco municipal swimming at its most photogenic.

A 1937 Art Deco lido nicknamed 'Brixton Beach,' ringed by Grade II-listed redbrick — a sun-trap of a summer institution between Herne Hill and Brixton. (Its operator's status has wobbled lately; check before you go.)

The Heath's spring-fed bathing ponds — Men's, Ladies' and Mixed — offer genuine wild swimming a few miles from the West End, among ducks and willow. Cold, clear and quietly addictive; lifeguarded in season.

A 50-metre heated outdoor pool kept around 25°C year-round and floodlit at dawn — Hackney's beloved Olympic-size lido, busy in summer, blissfully steaming in winter. The rare London lido you can happily swim in come January.

A roped-off swimming stretch of the Serpentine in Hyde Park, home to the Serpentine Swimming Club, who race for the Peter Pan Cup on Christmas morning. Open-water swimming in the very middle of the city.

Running beside the Coliseum off St Martin's Lane, Brydges Place is claimed to be London's narrowest alley — barely shoulder-wide for much of its length, opening to a single hidden bar at the far end. Turn sideways and squeeze through.

A narrow pedestrian lane between Pall Mall and King Street, lined with old-school shops, a sandwich bar and one of London's oldest pubs — a genuine time-warp slice of St James's the tourists walk straight past.

A gated private street that was the London estate of the Bishops of Ely and was long treated as a legal piece of Cambridgeshire — hiding St Etheldreda's, a rare 13th-century chapel, and (via Ely Court) the snug Ye Olde Mitre. A curiosity inside a curiosity.

q: Lovat Lane City of London cobbles
w: A steep, cobbled City lane with a central gutter — a rare survival of the medieval street pattern, curving between Eastcheap and the river past the spire of St Mary-at-Hill. Two minutes from the glass towers, six centuries away.

q: Christchurch Gardens Victoria Street plague pit
w: A pocket of green between Victoria Street and the buses, pressed into service as a plague burying ground in 1665 and later a churchyard — now lawns and benches where office workers eat lunch above the dead. A Suffragette memorial stands at its edge; the burials lie quietly beneath.

q: Bedlam burial ground Liverpool Street New Churchyard Crossrail
w: The 'New Churchyard' beside Bethlem hospital took some 25,000 Londoners from the 1560s — plague dead, paupers and the odd executed radical — until Crossrail's diggers reached them in the 2010s. A small commemorative space near Liverpool Street now marks one of the city's great forgotten cemeteries.

q: East Smithfield Black Death cemetery Royal Mint 1348
w: When the Black Death overwhelmed London in 1348 the city opened an emergency cemetery here by the Tower — neat rows of plague victims later excavated beneath the old Royal Mint, their bones yielding the DNA that confirmed Yersinia pestis. Walk the pavement above one of the pandemic's best-studied graves.

s: Thames-side pit, marked by a yew
q: All Saints Isleworth plague pit yew tree
w: In the riverside churchyard of All Saints, a dark old yew is said to mark a pit where Isleworth buried its plague dead. A quiet Thames-path detour where the village's losses lie under the grass beside the water.

s: Victorian prison vaults under a school
w: Beneath a Clerkenwell school survive the brick vaults of a Victorian remand prison that once held nearly 300 at a time — damp, echoing tunnels locals call the Clerkenwell Catacombs. Rarely open, and a favourite film set, but you walk right above it on the green.

q: Morpeth Arms Millbank prison cells ghost
w: A handsome 1845 riverside pub built for the warders of the vast Millbank Penitentiary — whose holding cells still lurk in the cellar, complete with a resident ghost the staff swear by. Drink a pint by the Thames where convicts once waited for transportation to Australia.

q: Viaduct Tavern Newgate prison cells gin palace
w: London's last great Victorian gin palace, all etched glass and beaten copper, sitting opposite the Old Bailey — and, the landlords will tell you, atop cells salvaged from Newgate Prison in its cellar. Ask nicely and you might see them; either way, admire one of the City's most ornate interiors.

q: St Bride's Fleet Street crypt charnel house bones
w: Wren's tiered 'wedding-cake' spire (said to have inspired the cake itself) crowns the journalists' church off Fleet Street — but the Blitz that gutted it also opened a crypt packed with centuries of bones, now a quietly macabre charnel house and museum. Printing history above, the dead below.

n: Museum of the Order of St John Crypt
q: Museum Order of St John Clerkenwell crypt St John's Gate
w: Behind St John's Gate, a rare survival: the 12th-century crypt of the Knights Hospitaller's English priory, thick-walled and solemn, holding medieval effigies and an alabaster Spanish knight. Crusader history hidden under quiet Clerkenwell, and free to visit.

q: Garden Museum St Mary-at-Lambeth crypt Tradescant Bligh tomb
w: The old church of St Mary-at-Lambeth, saved from demolition as the Garden Museum, hides a glass-floored crypt and a churchyard of remarkable tombs — the plant-hunter Tradescants and Captain Bligh of the Bounty among them. Horticulture, history and the dead, in the shadow of Lambeth Palace.

q: Whitefriars crypt Carmelite monastery Fleet Street Magpie Alley
w: All that remains of a great Carmelite friary that once sprawled between Fleet Street and the river: a single medieval stone undercroft, now improbably preserved in the basement of a modern office off Magpie Alley. A 700-year-old secret beneath the glass.

Opened in 1902 to replace the unreliable ferry for dock workers, this 370-metre cast-iron tunnel lets you walk directly beneath the Thames. Lined with 200,000 glazed white tiles, it is an echoing, surreal passage that feels completely detached from the city above — emerge on the Isle of Dogs for the classic Wren-framed view back at Greenwich.

Tucked inside Inigo Jones's Queen's House is the first geometric, self-supporting spiral staircase in Britain, built in the 1630s — the wrought-iron flowers in the balustrade give it its name. An architectural marvel, and famously the site of the 1966 'Rev. R. W. Hardy' ghost photograph that still draws the curious. Free to visit.

A Grade II listed chapel built in 1826 for retired pub landlords, bombed in the war and never fully restored. Today it stands as a beautifully weathered ruin — peeling paint, fractured stained glass, raw stone open to the light — and one of London's most photographed event spaces. Check listings; it opens for exhibitions and the odd ceremony.

Bazalgette's 1868 'Cathedral of Sewage' — a Byzantine-style temple of public health built to lift London's effluent into the great Northern Outfall sewer. Absurdly grand: cruciform plan, jewel-bright ironwork, Moorish detailing on a building for pumping waste. The exterior alone, glimpsed from the Greenway, is one of the city's strangest sights.

q: Aldwych ghost station Strand disused Underground
w: Closed in 1994, this 'ghost station' sheltered Londoners during the Blitz and hid the British Museum's Elgin Marbles from the bombs. Now preserved in amber and a favourite film set (Atonement, Sherlock). Interior access needs a rare Transport Museum tour, but the original oxblood-tiled façade on the Strand is a monument to London legend in its own right.

Jimi Hendrix lived at No. 23 (1968-69) right next door to where Handel composed Messiah at No. 25 — two English Heritage blue plaques side by side, now a joint museum. Hendrix cheerfully admitted he'd 'never heard much of the fella's stuff.'

q: Freddie Mercury house 22 Gladstone Avenue Feltham
w: The unassuming terrace where the Mercury family settled after fleeing Zanzibar in 1964; Freddie was living here when he met Brian May and Roger Taylor. The plaque was unveiled in 2016 by May and Freddie's sister — a true Queen-fan pilgrimage out west.

q: Charlie Chaplin plaque Glenshaw Mansions Brixton Road
w: The flat Chaplin shared with his brother Sydney (1908-10) at the very start of his career — a 'cherished haven' after one of the most brutal childhoods in Victorian London. Plaque unveiled by Paul Merton in 2017.

Where Christie wrote Murder on the Orient Express, Death on the Nile and The ABC Murders — three of the most-adapted mysteries ever written, all from one quiet house five minutes from Notting Hill Gate.

The cramped mews where Bacon lived and painted for over 30 years until 1992, in a legendary chaos of slashed canvases and paint-smeared walls — the room that produced Three Studies for a Crucifixion (the studio itself was later moved, brick by brick, to Dublin).
The Jamaican-British heroine of the Crimean War — who funded her own passage to nurse soldiers when the War Office turned her away — lived here in the 1850s. Her only known surviving London home.
The birthplace of the codebreaker and father of computer science, whose Bletchley Park work is credited with shortening the war by years — and who was then hounded to his death by the state he saved.

q: Napoleon III blue plaque King Street St James's
w: The oldest surviving official London blue plaque, and the only one ever installed in its subject's own lifetime — it bears the imperial eagle and marks the exiled emperor's London lodgings.

q: Douglas Adams grave Highgate Cemetery East
w: Fans leave pens and pencils in a pot before the plain dark headstone — a quiet running joke for the author of The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy. One of the cemetery's most-visited modern graves.

The Pop artist secretly designed his own headstone: the single laser-cut word 'DEAD' pierced clean through the stone in his own Art Deco lettering. Asked what his epitaph should read, he reputedly replied: 'Dead, of course.'

The Sex Pistols' manager lies under a bronze death mask, about 100 yards from Karl Marx, beneath the epitaph 'Better a spectacular failure, than a benign success' — the words of his old art-school teacher.

q: George Michael grave Highgate Cemetery West
w: In the family plot beside his mother and sister, the monument bears his birth name, Georgios Kyriacos Panayiotou. The West side is ticketed and tributes are gently discouraged — visit quietly and with respect.

q: Michael Faraday grave Highgate Cemetery West
w: A deliberately plain Sandemanian headstone for the self-taught giant of electromagnetism — who turned down a knighthood and burial in Westminster Abbey for exactly this kind of modesty.

q: George Wombwell lion tomb Highgate Cemetery
w: The Victorian travelling-menagerie owner is buried beneath a stone sculpture of his star attraction — Nero, the lion so gentle he famously refused to fight the baiting dogs set on him.

q: Bram Stoker ashes Golders Green Crematorium columbarium
w: The author of Dracula rests as ashes in an urn in the columbarium — viewable only by staff escort, a small final touch of gothic inaccessibility for the man who gave the world the Count.

q: Notting Hill blue door 280 Westbourne Park Road
w: The (now replica) blue door of William Thacker's flat from Notting Hill — the original was auctioned for charity after the film. Pure rom-com pilgrimage; mind the queue for a photo.
The cluttered antiques shop that plays Mr Gruber's in the Paddington films — and it's a genuine Portobello institution, every bit as eccentric off screen as on.

q: Chalcot Crescent Paddington Brown family house
w: A pastel-fronted house on a perfect curve of Primrose Hill stuccoes plays the Browns' home in the Paddington films. The whole crescent is an Instagram set-piece in its own right.
The real Belgravia townhouse used for the exterior shots of the classic series Upstairs, Downstairs — the front door behind which a whole world of Edwardian servants and masters was imagined.

q: The Clash debut album steps Stables Market Camden
w: The trolley ramp outside the old Rehearsal Rehearsals where The Clash were shot for their 1977 debut album cover. Punk pilgrims still line up to recreate the pose in the railway arches.

q: Battersea Power Station Pink Floyd Animals pig
w: The building on Pink Floyd's Animals sleeve — during the 1976 shoot the inflatable pig broke its moorings and drifted off over Kent, grounding flights at Heathrow. Now a vast redeveloped destination in its own right.

q: Word on the Water canal bookshop King's Cross
w: A 1920s Dutch barge moored on the canal, selling new and second-hand books from its decks with live jazz and poetry on the roof. One of the most photographed shops in London, and far lovelier than the photos.
Founded in 1797 and at 187 Piccadilly since 1810 — royal-warranted, arranged like a private townhouse over five creaking floors, and still the most civilised place in London to lose an afternoon.

A publisher and shop reviving neglected (mostly women) writers of the 20th century, each reissued in the same elegant dove-grey cover with a patterned endpaper. A quietly radical act of literary rescue.
A fiercely independent shop specialising in London and East End writing, running its own short story prize. Small, opinionated, and exactly the kind of place algorithms can't replace.

q: Libreria bookshop Hanbury Street no phones
w: A jewel-box shop by architects SelgasCano where books are shelved not by genre but by mood — 'Wanderlust', 'Enchantment for the Disenchanted' — under a strict no-phones policy designed to make you actually read.

q: Stanfords map travel bookshop Covent Garden Mercer Walk
w: The legendary travel and map shop, founded 1853 and name-checked in The Hound of the Baskervilles — where explorers from Scott to Livingstone bought their charts. Now on Mercer Walk, floors of maps and guides intact.
Founded in 1966 by poet John La Rose as Britain's first Black publisher and specialist bookshop — a cornerstone of Black British intellectual life and the Caribbean Artists Movement.

When Luigi Terroni opened here in 1878 it was, reputedly, the first delicatessen in all of England — set in old 'Little Italy' beside St Peter's Italian Church, and still slicing salami a century and a half on.

The first Italian church built outside Italy (1863), modelled on a basilica in Rome's Trastevere — still the heart of Little Italy, with its huge July Procession of Our Lady of Mount Carmel through the streets of Clerkenwell.

London's oldest surviving Ashkenazi synagogue, founded in 1854 by fifty poor Dutch-Jewish families inside a 1766 ex-Huguenot chapel — a building that has sheltered two waves of immigrants under one roof.

q: Bevis Marks Synagogue City of London
w: Britain's oldest synagogue in continuous use, consecrated 1701 by London's Sephardi community — its candlelit interior, lit by hanging brass chandeliers, has barely changed in over three centuries.

q: Windrush Square Brixton Black Cultural Archives
w: Renamed in 1998 for the 50th anniversary of the Empire Windrush, the square holds the UK's first African and Caribbean War Memorial and the Black Cultural Archives — the civic heart of Black Brixton.

The best-preserved early-Georgian street in Britain, built in the 1720s for Huguenot master silk-weavers — look up for the broad attic 'weavers' lights' that lit their looms, and for the lace-cuff hand door-knockers.

q: Brick Lane Jamme Masjid Fournier Street
w: One building that has been, in turn, a 1743 Huguenot chapel, a Methodist mission, a synagogue and now a mosque — the whole story of immigrant Spitalfields in one set of walls, beneath a sundial reading 'Umbra sumus' (We are shadows).
The Dalston grill where artists Gilbert & George have famously dined almost nightly for decades — a charcoal ocakbaşı as known for its surreal social-media presence as for its lamb.

q: Song Que Cafe Kingsland Road pho
w: The bright-green flagship of Kingsland Road's 'Pho Mile', drawing queues out the door since 2002. The bowl that taught a generation of Londoners what pho actually tastes like.

q: Lisboa Patisserie South Lambeth Road custard tart
w: The custard-tart heart of London's Portuguese community on South Lambeth Road — strong bica, warm pastéis de nata, and a counter that has anchored Little Portugal for decades.

q: Fish Wings and Tings Brixton Village
w: A tiny, much-loved Caribbean kitchen in Brixton Village from a Trinidadian chef — tamarind-glazed jerk wings and curried goat that helped make Brixton's covered market a dining destination.

q: ROA crane mural Hanbury Street Brick Lane
w: A roughly 40-foot bird painted in 2010 by the Belgian artist ROA — originally a heron, changed to a crane at the request of local Bengali residents, and now one of Brick Lane's most enduring works.

q: Banksy Guard Dog Rivington Street Shoreditch
w: A 2003 Banksy stencil of a security guard and his poodle, surviving behind perspex in the old Cargo courtyard alongside the HMV dog. Worth checking the courtyard is open before you make the trip.

q: Banksy royal family mural Stoke Newington Church Street
w: One of Banksy's best-preserved early works — a mock royal family that has waved cheerfully from a Stoke Newington wall since 2001, long before the auction-house millions.

q: Hackney Peace Carnival Mural Dalston Lane Ray Walker
w: Ray Walker's vivid 1985 mural of a multicultural anti-nuclear march down Dalston Lane — finished by his collaborators after his sudden death, and lovingly restored for its 40th anniversary.
A 2008 Banksy riff on a Spanish old master, restored to view on the corner of Portobello and Acklam Roads — a sly bit of art-history graffiti hiding in plain sight on the market's edge.

q: General Wolfe statue Greenwich Park view
w: From beside the Wolfe statue at the top of Greenwich Park, the sweep over the Old Royal Naval College to Canary Wharf and the City is so prized that the sightline is legally protected from obstruction.

q: Viktor Wynd Museum of Curiosities Hackney
w: A subterranean Wunderkabinett beneath The Last Tuesday Society bar — taxidermy, occult relics, dodo bones and infamous celebrity oddments, best explored with a cocktail in hand. London's strangest collection by some distance.

q: Novelty Automation Holborn arcade Tim Hunkin
w: Tim Hunkin's hand-built arcade of satirical coin-op machines — test your nerve against a nuclear reactor, take a 'instant holiday', or have your money laundered, literally. Joyful, anarchic and unlike anywhere else.

The world's first bricks-and-mortar museum dedicated to the gynaecological — crowdfunded, pun-loving, trans-inclusive and quietly radical, with rotating exhibitions that are far more educational than the name suggests.
The headquarters collection of the world's most famous society of magicians — including Houdini's own handcuffs — opened only for events and pre-booked tours behind a famously discreet door.

q: The Grapes Limehouse pub Narrow Street
w: A sliver of an 18th-century riverside pub on Narrow Street — now co-owned by Ian McKellen — that Dickens put into Our Mutual Friend as the Six Jolly Fellowship Porters. Step out the back for the Thames at your feet.

q: Hodge the Cat statue Gough Square Dr Johnson
w: A bronze of Dr Johnson's cat, perched on a dictionary with two oyster shells at his paws, outside the great lexicographer's house — 'a very fine cat indeed.' One of the city's 'Talking Statues.'

q: Sherlock Holmes statue Baker Street station
w: The deerstalkered detective in bronze outside Baker Street station, pipe in hand — and the most-listened-to of London's 'Talking Statues', if you call the number on the plaque.

s: The statue that sparked a row
q: Mary Wollstonecraft sculpture Newington Green Maggi Hambling
w: Maggi Hambling's 2020 silvered sculpture — a swirl of female forms topped by a tiny nude figure — on the green where the mother of feminism ran her school. Controversial from the moment it was unveiled, and still arguing its case.

q: City Wall at Vine Street Roman bastion exhibition
w: A ten-metre stretch of London's Roman and medieval wall, complete with a bastion, preserved in a basement gallery and opened to the public — free, climate-controlled, and astonishingly intact (book ahead).

Queens Park Rangers' home since 1917 — one of the smallest, steepest, loudest grounds in London football, hemmed in by terraced streets a few minutes from your front door. Officially the Kiyan Prince Foundation Stadium since 2019, renamed for a 15-year-old academy player killed outside his school in 2006, to keep his name alive.

Chelsea have played here since the club was founded in 1905 — the ground actually predates the team, built first with a club invented to fill it. Once an athletics stadium with a vast banked terrace; the Shed End behind the goal gave the club its noisiest support.

Arsenal's home since 2006, when they left nearby Highbury and its beloved Art Deco stands. A 60,000-seat bowl ringed with 'Arsenalisation' murals and the cannon crest; statues of Thierry Henry, Tony Adams and Dennis Bergkamp stand outside.

Opened in 2019 on the site of White Hart Lane, with a single-tier South Stand — the largest in the country — built for a sheer wall of noise. A retractable pitch slides away to reveal an artificial NFL surface beneath, and there's a microbrewery under the stands.

Built as the centrepiece of the 2012 Olympics — where Mo Farah and Jessica Ennis-Hill won gold — and now home to West Ham. The athletics track still rings the pitch, a sore point for fans who left the tight, fierce Upton Park in 2016.

Brentford's home since 2020, wedged beside the Kew Bridge railway and the Thames, replacing beloved Griffin Park — famous as the only English ground with a pub on all four corners. The Bees' climb from League One to the Premier League was one of football's best recent stories.

s: the national stadium under the arch
w: England's home, crowned by a 134-metre steel arch visible right across the city. The 2007 rebuild replaced the old Twin Towers where England won the 1966 World Cup; today it hosts cup finals, internationals and the biggest concerts London can stage.

Crystal Palace's home since 1924 and one of the most atmospheric grounds in the league — the Holmesdale Road end runs an ultras-style drum-and-banner show. Recently famous worldwide as AFC Richmond's 'dog pound' in Ted Lasso.

Home of Millwall, whose supporters wear their 'No one likes us, we don't care' chant as a badge of honour. The current Den opened in 1993 just up from the old one — a hard, proud South London club with deep dockland roots.

q: Speedy's Cafe North Gower Street NW1
w: The BBC's Sherlock barely touched Baker Street — 221B's front door is 187 North Gower Street, with Speedy's Sandwich Bar next door playing itself. The cafe still does a roaring trade in fans ordering the 'Sherlock wrap'.

s: the Harry Potter trolley in the wall
w: A luggage trolley vanishing into the brickwork, marking where Harry caught the Hogwarts Express. The photo-op and its shop sit in the King's Cross concourse — queue for a scarf and a wand-flick, mid-leap, for the camera.

q: St Luke's Mews Notting Hill W11
w: A cobbled, pastel-painted mews where Mark (Andrew Lincoln) held up his silent cue cards to Juliet (Keira Knightley) in Love Actually. Number 27 is still pink; a sign asks a pound per camera for charity. Just off Portobello Road.

The real headquarters of the Secret Intelligence Service — the cream-and-green ziggurat on the river that Bond films love to blow up, most memorably in Skyfall and Spectre. Best seen from Vauxhall Bridge or the far bank.

This red-brick Georgian villa on the edge of Greenwich Park is the exterior of Bridgerton House — though the wisteria-draped gates are pure TV magic. Inside is the Wernher Collection of medieval and Renaissance treasures; the park beyond stood in for Mayfair.

The 300-year-old pub on Richmond Green that plays the Crown & Anchor in Ted Lasso (the interior is a studio rebuild). Step out and down Paved Court to find Ted's front door at number 11a — '9 3/4' on screen.

s: Bridget Jones's flat, above the pub
q: The Globe Tavern Bedale Street Borough
w: On the edge of Borough Market, this is the pub over which Bridget Jones supposedly kept her flat — the camera looks down on the market from her window. The opening 'All By Myself' singalong made the spot a fan favourite.

s: a real-life TARDIS on the street
w: A genuine 1996 Metropolitan Police call box stands outside Earl's Court Tube — the same blue Mark 2 design the BBC turned into Doctor Who's TARDIS. Whovians treat the unassuming corner as a quiet shrine.

Deep in Richmond Park’s deer-cropped acres hides a 40-acre woodland garden that detonates into azalea fire every late April. Lord Sidmouth fenced it off two centuries ago to keep the deer out; now its streams and ponds glow with rhododendron and Kurume azaleas brought from Japan in the 1920s. Few bother to seek it — wander the paths and let the colour do its quiet violence.

A narrow gullet off the old Petticoat Lane, named for the frying pans that ironmongers once hung above their doors as trade signs. Step in and the centuries press close — East End grit gone quiet. The sort of passage you crave on an afternoon when you want alleys that whisper instead of shout.

s: ironworks turned wild · a Lea peninsula
w: On a peninsula where the River Lea coils toward the Thames, an 1846 ironworks and coal wharf has been reclaimed by reeds, ponds and birdsong. The DLR rattles overhead; tidal water laps below. A Green Flag wildlife haven hiding a thousand feet from Canning Town station — proof the city still grows wild at its edges.

A triangular plot at the southern tip of Hackney Marshes, planted with 30,000 trees in 1996 when locals fought off the developers. Silver birches reach up with a faintly ghostly hush; plane trees lean in from the towpath. The busy road runs close, yet the wood swallows the noise. London still has wild edges worth defending.

A crooked City street curling past St Bartholomew’s, named for the Dukes of Brittany who once kept houses here. The roar of the Square Mile fades to a whisper between the old walls; duck through toward Smithfield and half-expect a Dickens character to round the corner. Medieval London, still legible if you slow down.

A short cobbled descent from Richmond’s bustle down to the river, lined with the backs of old buildings. At the bottom the Thames opens out — boats, willows, evening light. An atmospheric little throat of a lane, the kind you take slowly with nowhere in particular to be.

A 1535 Tudor manor of warm red brick, marooned among modern Hackney — manor, then school, then in the 1980s a squatted punk den whose graffiti the National Trust chose to keep. Wander the panelled rooms and quiet courtyard and the centuries fold together: oak linenfold, Jacobean painted shields, and a rare survivor’s stubborn calm. A time machine off Homerton High Street.

s: a Victorian writer’s home · walled garden
w: On a quiet Chelsea row built in 1708, the home of Thomas and Jane Carlyle survives almost unchanged — dim hallways, the decoupage screen Jane made in 1849, the small walled garden where the great Victorian wrote and fretted. Dickens, Tennyson and Ruskin all climbed these stairs. A modest house, thick with the texture of a literary life.

A cobbled Deptford side street, laid out in 1706 for dockyard officers and shipwrights, that has somehow kept its early-Georgian terrace intact. Look up at the doorways — carved wooden canopies, each bracket a different design, cut by ship’s carpenters. Look down at the cobbles. At the far end, the spire of St Paul’s. London as it once imagined itself: orderly, durable, slow.

q: Ronnie Scott’s Jazz Club Frith Street
w: Down a Frith Street doorway glows the red sign of Britain’s most storied jazz club, open since 1959. Miles, Monk and Hendrix all played here — the last gig Hendrix ever performed was on this stage. Velvet dark, low-lit and reverent: the kind of room where you order a drink, stop talking, and let the music have the floor.

Frank Matcham’s 1901 music hall on Mare Street, all gilt and terracotta swagger, where Marie Lloyd and Charlie Chaplin once worked the variety bill and stand-ups still do. The ornate auditorium still crackles with the ghosts of a thousand turns — a temple to performance that somehow dodged the wrecking ball. Magnificent, lit up at night.

A century-old Dalston street market where the city’s appetites collide — yams and plantain, Turkish bread, Caribbean fish counters, bolts of fabric, gospel and the shouts of traders. Loud, cheap, gloriously alive and stubbornly itself amid the creeping cafes. Come hungry, wander slowly, and eat where the queue is longest.

n: Our Lady Mother of the Church Polish Church
w: Former Ealing Broadway Methodist church (1868 Gothic Revival with tall spire) acquired by the Polish community in 1986. Part of the quiet westward drift of Polonia from Acton through Ealing Common. Now home to Polish Roman Catholic parish life — services, memory, community in a building that outlived its original congregation. A pilgrimage site for West London Poles.
q: Polish Church Ealing Our Lady Mother of the Church

n: Rubens at the Palace (Polish Government-in-Exile HQ)
w: During the war this hotel at 39 Buckingham Palace Road housed the Polish Government-in-Exile’s military headquarters. From here Sikorski and others coordinated the fight, intelligence and diplomacy while a state that refused to die operated from London. Plaques and memory remain — the paper trail of exile in plain sight near the palace gardens.
q: Rubens Hotel Buckingham Palace Road Polish exile

n: Polish Air Force Memorial, St Clement Danes
w: St Clement Danes, rebuilt after the Blitz as the Central Church of the Royal Air Force. The floor is inlaid with over a thousand hand-carved Welsh slate badges of RAF squadrons and units. Specific memorials honour the Polish Air Force — the men who flew with the RAF from British soil during the war, many never to return home. Ties directly into the wider story of Polish exile, contribution and memory.
q: Polish Air Force Memorial St Clement Danes London

w: A radical, community-run arts centre and theatre in Deptford with deep roots in 1970s–80s alternative culture, anti-racism work and local organising. Still going strong with a programme that mixes experimental performance, music and community projects. A living piece of South London’s grassroots cultural history in a former church hall.

Legendary Islington pub that became one of the key venues of the late-1970s punk and new wave explosion. The Stranglers, The Damned, Joy Division and countless others played the tiny back room. Still a proper music pub with history you can feel in the walls. One of the last places where that raw, pre-corporate music scene feels tangible.

The oldest continuously working film studio in the world and the spiritual home of the great Ealing Comedies (Kind Hearts and Coronets, The Lavender Hill Mob, Passport to Pimlico). The lot, stages and offices where Alec Guinness, Stanley Holloway and so many others created a very particular, very British kind of cinema. Still active, still full of ghosts.

The extraordinary 17th-century tomb of John Tradescant the Elder and Younger — royal gardeners, plant hunters and collectors of curiosities — in the churchyard of St Mary-at-Lambeth. Carved with exotic plants, animals and the family crest. The men who brought the pineapple, the horse chestnut and countless other species to Britain. A strange, beautiful monument to curiosity and empire.

A proper old Highgate village pub with 17th-century origins and a long list of famous (and infamous) drinkers — from highwaymen to writers and actors. Low ceilings, wood panelling, and the feeling that the village that once stood here is still just outside the door. One of the last places where old Highgate survives.

The excavated remains of the Rose — the first purpose-built Elizabethan playhouse on Bankside and the main rival to the later Globe. Visible through a glass floor in the basement of a modern office building. The stage where Marlowe’s plays were first performed and where the very first Shakespeare productions happened. A genuine archaeological ghost of Elizabethan theatre.

Family bakery started in 1911 by Ukrainian refugee Hyman Rinkoff and his wife in Stepney. Passed through generations, still baking breads, cakes, pastries and legendary jam doughnuts from Eastern European recipes. A living piece of East End immigrant food history that has fed the community for over a century.

Classic art deco working man’s café with Formica tables, chrome and proper strong tea. Big full English breakfasts and a no-nonsense atmosphere. Has appeared in films but remains a genuine local caff. One of the best-preserved mid-century working-class eating places in London.

A proper traditional pie and mash shop in Dalston that has been feeding generations of East Londoners. Stewed eels, liquor, mash and the classic tiled interior. A living working-class food institution that feels increasingly rare. Strong tea and no fuss — exactly as it should be.
q: F Cooke pie and mash Dalston London

w: The UK’s only St Lucian bakery in Dalston — family-run and carrying Caribbean recipes and traditions into East London. Fresh bakes, community warmth and the feeling of a neighbourhood institution that belongs to the people who live here. A beautiful, specific piece of London’s immigrant food story.

Proper old-school London bakery on Essex Road since 1948. Fresh bread, crusty rolls, sweet and savoury bakes made the traditional way. The kind of place where quality has never been compromised and locals still queue for the real thing. A living piece of mid-century Islington food culture.
q: Raabs The Bakers Essex Road Islington

w: A proper East End market café on Chrisp Street that still serves pease pudding — a dish with medieval roots once common in working-class London eateries. Family-run, no fuss, and one of the last places keeping this specific piece of London food heritage alive.
q: Ivy's Cafe Chrisp Street Poplar London

w: One of the very few buildings in the City that survived the Great Fire of 1666. A timber-framed pub with centuries of history. The interior still carries the feeling of pre-Fire London. A quiet survivor in the shadow of the modern financial district.

A beautiful tiled Victorian pub in the heart of Bloomsbury with a long association with writers, academics and the Bloomsbury Group. Etched glass snob screens and a peaceful back room. Still a proper local with deep cultural roots.

Built for the workmen who constructed St Bride’s Church after the Great Fire. A proper old Fleet Street boozer with a long association with journalists and printers. Quiet, slightly hidden and full of the layered history of the street.
q: Old Bell Tavern Fleet Street London

w: A proper Shoreditch caff that has held its corner through every wave of reinvention around it — strong tea, an unfussy plate and the easy regulars'-table feel that keeps a changing street human.

A genuine Archway working man's caff — pork sausage and bacon, bubble and squeak, mugs of strong tea and the unhurried local conversation that has kept places like this going while the chains came and went.
A much-loved old greasy spoon between Tulse Hill and West Norwood — big breakfasts, proper tea and the unhurried calm of a real local that has quietly fed the area for years.
A friendly Hackney Wick local doing lentil soup, sandwiches and reliable home-style cooking — one of those small, steady anchors that holds a fast-changing neighbourhood together.
A family-run Hackney bakery turning out some of the best croissants and pastries in the area — daily, early and made with real care. The kind of unflashy local a street organises its mornings around.

Built in 1888, this was the first market street in London lit by electricity — cast-iron canopies and incandescent bulbs that let traders work after dark while the rest of the city still squinted by gaslight. It gave Eddy Grant his 1983 anthem, written in the wake of the 1981 Brixton uprising, and still carries the African and Caribbean produce stalls at the heart of Brixton Market.

A scruffy 1970s estate pub beside Windmill Gardens that became one of the most important small venues in the country. Through the late 2010s it incubated the South London guitar scene — Black Country, New Road, Squid, Fat White Family and black midi all cut their teeth on its tiny stage. Not to be confused with the heritage windmill a hundred metres away; this Windmill grinds something louder.

Christopher Marlowe — Shakespeare’s exact contemporary, rival, and probable government spy — was buried in an unmarked grave here on 1 June 1593, days after being stabbed through the eye in a Deptford lodging house, in a killing that still smells of the Walsingham intelligence network. The medieval tower survives; the rest was rebuilt in 1697. The carved skull-and-crossbones on the churchyard gateposts are said to have given the world the pirate’s Jolly Roger.

The site of the most celebrated private garden in 17th-century England, laid out by the diarist John Evelyn from 1652 — French parterres and a legendary holly hedge. In 1698 Evelyn lent the house to Tsar Peter the Great, then in Deptford learning shipbuilding; the Tsar and his men wrecked the place, reportedly by barrowing one another through the hedge, and the Treasury paid out over £350 in damages. A quiet park and an ancient mulberry mark the spot — and it was very nearly the National Trust’s first-ever acquisition.

A lush pocket garden created by residents in 1990 on a WWII bomb site, named for the grand Vauxhall Pleasure Gardens that once lay just to the north. The 1870s railway-workers’ square was saved from demolition by 1980s squatters, who turned it into a housing co-op with a vegetarian café and a wholefood shop; designer Dan Pearson planted the garden, threading English borders through tropical exotics around a salvaged industrial waterwheel.

The stretch of South Lambeth Road between Vauxhall and Stockwell is the heart of the UK’s largest Portuguese-speaking community — over nine thousand in Lambeth alone — many with roots in Madeira and arriving from the 1960s on. Red-and-green pastelarias like Lisboa and Estrela serve pastéis de nata, salt cod and bica strong enough to walk you home. London’s quietest, most delicious bit of saudade.
q: Little Portugal South Lambeth Road Stockwell

w: Founded in 1977 on demolition-scarred land, this is one of London’s oldest and most central city farms — pigs, sheep, goats and alpacas grazing barely a stone’s throw from the MI6 building on the river. On a sliver of inner-city acreage it runs youth and education programmes and a Riding for the Disabled centre. Espionage on one bank, alpacas on the other.

A free contemporary-art gallery in a purpose-built hall that opened in 1891, founded by William Rossiter to bring art to the people of South London — with Frederic Leighton, Burne-Jones and Walter Crane among its early backers and Gladstone as first president. In 2018 it spread across the road into a Grade II-listed former fire station. Ambitious, unstuffy and still free, more than a century on.

The old Regent’s Canal Dock, opened in 1820 as the link between London’s canal network and the Thames — once a coal-and-timber port thick with barges and lighters, now a glassy yacht marina ringed by Docklands towers. From here the Limehouse Cut (1770), London’s oldest canal, strikes north to the Lea. Stand on the lock and you’re at the hinge between the inland canals and the tidal river.

On an island in the River Lea stands the House Mill of 1776 — one of the largest tidal mills left in the world, Grade I listed. It worked the rise and fall of the Lea to grind grain for an adjacent gin distillery, the tide doing for free what steam later charged for. A startlingly rural survivor in the shadow of the Olympic Park.

Built in 1695 by Trinity House for ‘28 decay’d Masters & Commanders of Ships or ye Widows of such’, on land given by a Ratcliffe sea-captain. Two rows of cottages face a quiet green, model warships perched on the gable ends; C.R. Ashbee saved them from demolition in 1895. A pocket of the maritime 17th century stranded on the Mile End Road.

Founded around 1695 by the Levant Company merchant Sir John Morden for fellow traders ruined ‘by perils of the seas’ — a serene Baroque red-brick quadrangle long credited to Wren, though actually raised by his master mason Edward Strong. Still a working retirement community on the edge of Blackheath. Charity built on the risk of the sea, three centuries before bankruptcy law.

A trim Georgian courtyard of 1746–52, built under the will of the fishmonger Charles Hopton to house ‘poor decayed men’ of the parish — and now hidden directly behind the roar of Tate Modern. Twenty-six little brick houses around a lawn, almost absurdly peaceful given the Turbine Hall is a minute’s walk away.

Fourteen almshouses of 1714, built by the Ironmongers’ Company from the bequest of Sir Robert Geffrye — a merchant whose fortune was partly bound up in the transatlantic slave trade, a fact the museum now confronts head-on. Saved by the London County Council in 1911 and opened as a museum in 1914, it walks you through four centuries of the English living room, with a restored almshouse interior on select days.
q: Museum of the Home Geffrye Hoxton

w: Founded in 1613 by Henry Howard, Earl of Northampton, for twenty poor men, and dressed up in toy-Gothic in 1812 — a tiny crenellated almshouse dwarfed by the old power station beside it on the Thames Path. It sits almost exactly on the Greenwich Meridian, run to this day by the Mercers’ Company. Easy to miss, impossible to forget.

A breathtaking vaulted brick subway of 1865 by Charles Barry Jr., built to carry first-class passengers beneath the road to the High Level station serving the relocated Crystal Palace — red and cream brick fanning overhead like a Byzantine crypt. The palace burned in 1936; this jewel of a passage survived underground, opened now only on occasional days.

One of just thirteen surviving green-painted Victorian cabmen’s shelters — of sixty-one built between 1875 and 1950 — each by law no larger than a horse and cart so as not to block the road. Inside, a tiny kitchen still serves tea and bacon to black-cab drivers, a members’ club the size of a garden shed. All thirteen are now listed.

The bookshop of the ‘London Review of Books’, opened in 2003 a step from the British Museum — densely, intelligently stocked, with an adjoining cake shop that regulars rate almost as highly as the shelves. The natural habitat of the Bloomsbury reader, and one of the best events programmes in the city.

A radical and peace-movement bookshop founded in 1945 and named for the pacifist poet Laurence Housman, on the Caledonian Road near King’s Cross. Shelves of dissent, small-press politics and second-hand bargains in the basement; it has outlasted nearly every other bookshop of its kind in Britain.

The only original Georgian livery hall left in the City, built 1778–80 by William Blackburn for the Company of Watermen and Lightermen — the Thames boatmen who were London’s original cabbies. Uniquely it is a guild ‘without livery’, created and governed by Act of Parliament in 1555. A jewel-box of a hall down a lane near the river; interior by tour only.

The oldest surviving livery hall in the City, rebuilt by 1672 after the Great Fire on the site of a Dominican priory. The Society of Apothecaries founded the Chelsea Physic Garden in 1673 and made and sold drugs here into the 20th century; a young John Keats qualified to practise medicine within these walls in 1816. Glimpse the courtyard from Black Friars Lane.

The opulent 1835 hall of one of the City’s ‘Great Twelve’ companies, by Philip Hardwick — and the literal origin of the word ‘hallmark’, for here silver and gold have been assayed and marked since the 14th century. The annual Trial of the Pyx still tests the nation’s coinage under its roof.

The Palladian official residence of the Lord Mayor of London, built 1739–52 by George Dance the Elder — home to the barrel-vaulted Egyptian Hall and a world-class hoard of Dutch Golden Age paintings. The grandest civic house in the Square Mile, facing the Bank and the old Royal Exchange across the busiest junction in the City.

A curved Streamline-Moderne block of 1936 on Charterhouse Square, with a roof garden and basement pool — and, to a generation of television viewers, Hercule Poirot’s ‘Whitehaven Mansions’. Its sleek sun-trap balconies wrap the corner like the prow of a liner run aground in Clerkenwell.

A rare 1931 Art Deco multi-storey car garage on Herbrand Street, by Wallis, Gilbert & Partners — the same hand as the Hoover Building — its great curved corner of Crittall glazing wrapped around a sculptural internal spiral ramp. Built for the age of the chauffeured Daimler, now offices, still unmistakably itself.

Wallis, Gilbert & Partners’ dazzling ‘Modern Palace of Industry’ of 1933 on the A40 — sunburst tiles, Egyptian and Aztec colour, a façade built to be admired at speed from a passing car. Betjeman called it ‘a sort of Art Deco Wentworth Woodhouse’; it’s flats now, with a supermarket behind, but the front still stops the traffic.

Charles Holden’s monumental stone tower of 1937 for the University of London — stripped-classical, faintly sinister, and the acknowledged model for the Ministry of Truth in Orwell’s ‘Nineteen Eighty-Four’ (the wartime Ministry of Information really was based here). London’s first true skyscraper in spirit if not in height.

A vast Art Deco super-cinema of 1933 by George Coles on the Commercial Road — 3,500 seats, a Wurlitzer and a ceiling like a sunset. Later a Mecca bingo hall and, improbably, the Royal Opera House’s rehearsal studio; now a restored events palace, the grandest surviving picture-house of the East End.

Twenty-two miles of man-made chalk and flint tunnels beneath Kent's edge — a WWI munitions store, a 1920s mushroom farm, then London's largest deep air-raid shelter, sheltering 15,000 with a chapel and a hospital during the Blitz. The only child born down here was christened Rose Cavena Wakeman. In the 50s and 60s it turned music venue: Bowie played four times, plus Hendrix, Pink Floyd and the Yardbirds. Guided tours only.

s: Tudor bricks in the shopping street
w: Built in 1596 by Archbishop John Whitgift as a hospital for the elderly, and still a working almshouse for around a dozen residents — a knot of Tudor brick hiding in plain sight amid the glass shopfronts of Croydon's commercial heart. The courtyard opens to the public during Open House; the rest of the year you simply walk past four centuries without noticing.

A 17th-century chalk-and-flint chequer house beside Carshalton Ponds, possibly built as a commercial cold-bath house over the spring outflows. Now Sutton's local-history museum, with an Edwardian billiard room and a room devoted to vanished Nonsuch Palace. The water still rises where it always did, under the floor.

q: Little Holland House Frank Dickinson Carshalton
w: Self-built between 1902 and 1904 by Frank Dickinson, a working-class disciple of Ruskin and Morris, who made the house and almost everything in it by hand — the furniture, the paintings, the metalwork, the carvings. An entire Arts and Crafts philosophy executed by one man on a suburban plot. Grade II; open first Sundays.

q: Bowie Bandstand Croydon Road Recreation Ground Beckenham
w: A Grade II-listed cast-iron bandstand of 1905, believed the only surviving example by McCallum & Hope of Glasgow. On 16 August 1969 David Bowie compered and played the Beckenham Arts Lab free festival here, five days after burying his father; it became 'Memory of a Free Festival'. The Arts Lab had started in the back room of the Three Tuns up the High Street — now a Zizzi.

q: Cah Chi New Malden Korean barbecue
w: New Malden holds an estimated 10,000 ethnic Koreans — and, per CNN, some hundreds of North Korean defectors, the largest such community in Europe, seeded in the 1970s when the South Korean ambassador and Samsung's HQ settled nearby. Cah Chi is the long-running family barbecue institution at its heart: charcoal-grilled meat, soju, and the kind of cooking the diaspora actually eats.

s: the community hub behind the café
q: Korean Culture and Arts Centre New Malden High Street
w: A modest community hub and function room behind a white-walled café on the High Street — the cultural engine room of New Malden's Korean diaspora, where the events, classes and gatherings happen that the restaurants only hint at. The unglamorous heart of Europe's Little Korea.

q: St Peter's Church Petersham Vancouver grave
w: A church of Saxon origin, in Domesday, that Pevsner loved for an interior the Victorians never touched: Georgian box pews and a two-decker pulpit, frozen before the great refit. Captain George Vancouver — who charted the Pacific Northwest and gave his name to the city — lies in the churchyard, a long way from the coast he mapped.

q: Ham House ghost spaniel Duchess of Lauderdale
w: A 1610 Stuart mansion famous for its cantilevered Great Staircase and the Duchess of Lauderdale's private 'bathing roome'. Her ghostly King Charles spaniel is still said to pad the halls — a visitor once complained about a dog she'd seen indoors, and was told no real dog is ever allowed in. One of the least-altered seventeenth-century houses in the country.

q: Gurdwara Sri Guru Singh Sabha Southall
w: London's largest Sikh temple, opened in March 2003 at a cost of £17.5m raised entirely from the local Sikh community, and visited by the then-Prince Charles that June. Marble prayer halls, gold and white, and free langar served all day to anyone who walks in, regardless of faith. The civic heart of Southall.

Founded in 1965 by Mohammed Ali Khan and named for his Haryana birthplace — the first shop opened in Drummond Street, among the very first South Asian confectioners in Britain. Trays of barfi, jalebi and laddoo glow under the lights. Khan died in 2023; in 2025 the chain was bought out, 22 stores strong, but the original counters still pull the Diwali queues.

q: London Museum of Water and Steam Kew Bridge
w: The former Kew Bridge Pumping Station, home to the world's largest collection of Cornish beam engines. The Grand Junction 90-inch was once the largest working beam engine on earth — Dickens called it 'a monster' — and the 100-inch is the biggest single-cylinder beam engine left anywhere. On steaming days the great beams still rock overhead, slow as breathing.

A few doors from the steam museum, a building full of machines that play themselves — a theremin, self-playing pianos, orchestrions, and a mighty Wurlitzer that once rose on its lift to soundtrack a west London cinema and still does, for visitors. Music made by ghosts and gears.

Sir John Soane designed and built this Regency villa between 1800 and 1804 as a country retreat and a school for the architectural dynasty his sons flatly refused to become. He added mock-Roman ruins to the grounds and fished the ponds with a young J.M.W. Turner. The dream died; the house, beautifully restored, did not.

q: Battle of Britain Bunker Uxbridge No 11 Group
w: The Grade I-listed underground operations room of No. 11 Group Fighter Command, sixty feet down, from which the Battle of Britain and later the D-Day air cover were directed. Churchill visited in September 1940 and, on the way out, reputedly first said the line about so much being owed by so many to so few. Tours only; the map table is still set.

q: Battle of Britain House ruins Ruislip Woods
w: Overgrown garden steps and concrete foundations in Ruislip Woods are all that survive of a mansion, burned in 1984, reputedly used to train clandestine agents for occupied France. Nature is quietly reclaiming the formal terraces. You can walk straight past the place where people were taught to disappear.

An ancient linear earthwork running some six miles from Cuckoo Hill to Pear Wood, still up to two metres high, wrapped in unproven legends of Boudica's last stand and a 'Boadicea's Grave' on Stanmore Common. The Grim's Dyke estate beside it housed the eccentric W.S. Gilbert and his menagerie of lemurs, monkeys and a lynx.

A pond on Stanmore's Little Common, near the site of the Roman station Sulloniacae, where local myth insists Julius Caesar tasted the water during his conquest. He almost certainly didn't — but the name has stuck for centuries, which is its own kind of truth.

A forgotten network of Victorian chalk-mining tunnels, up to twenty metres deep, that announced itself in 2017 when a sinkhole opened at Pinner Wood School, shutting it for nine months while engineers grouted the voids. Generations of children had been taught directly over the dark.

s: the flying saucer that landed in 1933
w: Charles Holden's streamline-moderne masterpiece — Googie before Googie existed — a circular building whose flat roof seems to float on a band of windows, crowned by a 'Tesla coil' finial that local lore (apocryphally) says inspired the Daleks. Grade II*; the original Art Deco clock opposite was restored in 2023.

s: the lost Tudor palace next door
w: A Grade I Jacobean manor built around 1630 for Lord Mayor Sir Nicholas Rainton, who was jailed in the Tower in 1640 for defying Charles I. In the grounds lie the buried, scheduled remains of Elsyng Palace, where the future Edward VI and Elizabeth I were reportedly told of Henry VIII's death in 1547 — excavated most years, and shown on Digging for Britain.

q: Myddelton House Gardens E A Bowles Enfield
w: The lifelong garden of plantsman E.A. 'Gussie' Bowles, who coined the word galanthophile. His 'Lunatic Asylum' is a bed of deliberately deformed plants — corkscrew hazel, contorted forms, botanical misfits. The New River once cut through here, filled in the late 1960s with spoil from the Victoria line dig, its 1832 footbridge left stranded under a wisteria Bowles grew from seed in 1903.

q: Trent Park M Room German generals MI6
w: A WWII secret-listening site where 59 captured German generals were held in luxury — Harrods trips, fine dining, a fake aristocrat to flatter them — while microphones hidden in the walls, fireplaces and garden benches fed three basement 'M Rooms', staffed by German-speaking Jewish refugees under MI6's Colonel Kendrick. The transcripts captured some of the earliest evidence of the Holocaust. A museum has been announced — check before you go.

The fossil-, crystal- and shell-lined ruin of an eighteenth-century grotto-boathouse, built in the 1760s for two thousand pounds — the last grand survival of vanished Wanstead House, once one of England's grandest Palladian mansions. Gutted by fire in 1884 when the lake had been drained for cleaning, it now stands fenced, on the Heritage at Risk register.

q: The Champions statue Bobby Moore Green Street West Ham
w: Bobby Moore, Geoff Hurst and Martin Peters hoisting the Jules Rimet trophy in bronze at 'Morley's Corner' — the West Ham trio at the heart of England's 1966 World Cup win, frozen mid-celebration. Beside the Boleyn Tavern, where Gandhi reputedly drank cream soda and talked football in 1931.

s: Little India of the East End
q: Green Street Queens Market Upton Park saris
w: A South Asian retail corridor of saris, gold and fabric, anchored by the covered Queens Market — and the historic boundary between East and West Ham. The lost Green Street House nearby, 'Boleyn Castle', wrapped in Anne Boleyn legend, gave West Ham the castle on their crest.

q: City of London Cemetery Manor Park catacomb valley
w: A vast Grade I-registered Victorian cemetery, laid out in the 1850s by William Haywood on the former Aldersbrook estate — serpentine drives, a formally planted sunken Catacomb Valley, and the quiet grandeur of a place built to bury a whole city. One of the largest municipal cemeteries in Europe, and still working.

n: The Dial Arch & Royal Brass Foundry
q: Dial Arch Royal Arsenal Woolwich Arsenal FC
w: The Royal Arsenal — once a 1,300-acre 'Secret City' employing some 80,000 in the First World War — survives as twenty-odd listed buildings. Engineers at the Dial Arch, now a pub, founded Dial Square FC in 1886; it became Arsenal. The 1717 Royal Brass Foundry, where the empire's cannon were cast, still stands nearby.

A roll-on, roll-off ferry across the Thames, free of all tolls by an Act of 1885 and running since 1889 — one of the last places you cross the river for nothing, cars and foot passengers together. A 2018 vessel is named for Ben Woollacott, a nineteen-year-old crewman who died on the job in 2011.

s: dig for shark teeth by the ruins
q: Lesnes Abbey fossil pit Abbey Wood
w: The ruins of a twelfth-century Augustinian abbey, founded in 1178 by Richard de Lucy — possibly in penance for the murder of Becket — beside a Site of Special Scientific Interest where the public may dig for fifty-five-million-year-old shark teeth and Eocene fossils. A mulberry planted around 1620 still fruits among the stones.

s: the man standing in the Thames
q: The Diver Regeneration John Kaufman Rainham riverside
